top_findings.finding: Participant expressed lack of confidence in choosing a healthcare assessment due to excessive cognitive effort required to compare all options, and indicated intention to use an external tool (ChatGPT) instead of completing the task within the product.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant stated they would not have enough confidence to choose and would need to use ChatGPT to compare assessments rather than working through the product interface.
top_findings.stated_signal: I personally don't think I'd have enough confidence to choose a healthcare assessment because I think it would take me a really long time. To kind of get through all of that and I could see myself maybe go to like chat GPT and saying like can you compare Bupa's health assessments, um. And let me know which one has this and this.
top_findings.rationale: The participant acknowledged the task is theoretically possible but the excessive effort required to compare multiple healthcare assessments caused them to abandon the in-product pathway in favour of an external workaround. Both observed behaviour (stated intention to leave the product) and stated signal (explicit concern about time and effort) indicate the component task of selecting an assessment is blocked without external intervention, matching S2 and L4.

top_findings.finding: Participant experienced visual discomfort and aversion to the page design due to excessive brightness and color contrast, stating intention to close the page rather than engage with content.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant expressed reluctance to read the page, stated intention to close it down, and later complained about eye strain from bright white and green colors.
top_findings.stated_signal: because the the page is so bright, I probably, I probably would just be like, OK, I really don't wanna read that, um, and so I just close that page down. my eyes are hurting just from looking at it.
top_findings.rationale: The participant experienced accessibility friction related to visual design—specifically brightness and color contrast causing eye strain and avoidance behaviour. Both stated discomfort ('my eyes are hurting') and observed avoidance behaviour (intent to close the page) indicate significant friction that interferes with content engagement, matching S3 and L3 (significant friction with barrier being worked around or skipped).
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Participants with visual sensitivity, light sensitivity, or certain visual impairments are structurally amplified by excessive brightness and high-contrast colour combinations.

top_findings.finding: Participant experiences significant visual strain and readability friction from bright white text on the website, which conflicts with their accessibility settings and causes them to avoid reading the content.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant expressed strong distress about bright white lettering multiple times ('Oh God, the bright white. Oh my God'), described avoiding reading it ('there's absolutely, there's no way I'm reading that'), and explained they use dark mode and nighttime mode to mitigate the issue.
top_findings.stated_signal: The lettering is really bright. I much prefer something a bit more neutral. There's absolutely, there's no way I'm reading that. The bright white is really frustrating.
top_findings.rationale: The participant's stated inability to read bright white text and observed reliance on browser-level workarounds (dark mode, nighttime mode, color inversion) indicate that the interface's colour contrast and brightness settings do not meet accessibility requirements. The participant skipped or avoided reading content due to visual strain, constituting a significant accessibility barrier that was worked around rather than resolved by the product design.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Users with visual sensitivities, photophobia, or light-sensitivity conditions experience structurally higher friction from high-brightness colour schemes. The participant's need to enable multiple browser-level accessibility features indicates that the product's default styling creates barriers for this cohort.

top_findings.finding: Participant reported difficulty reading white text on dark blue background and stated she would avoid using that section due to visual contrast problems.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant described the contrast as 'really difficult for my eyes to like adjust to' and explicitly stated 'I probably just wouldn't use it' when referring to the white-on-dark-blue section.
top_findings.stated_signal: white on the dark blue is like, yeah, really difficult for my eyes to like adjust to and then here... I probably just wouldn't use it.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly identified an accessibility friction caused by insufficient contrast between text and background colour. She stated she would skip or avoid using this section entirely, indicating significant accessibility friction that results in barrier avoidance rather than task completion through that pathway.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Contrast sensitivity issues are a structural accessibility barrier affecting users with low vision or visual processing differences. This friction is amplified for users who rely on sufficient contrast ratios.

top_findings.finding: Participant identified a wayfinding issue with the accessibility label, suggesting that non-English speakers would not find language support services under the word 'accessibility'.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant paused to reflect on the information architecture and suggested that the accessibility section might not be discoverable by the target audience.
top_findings.stated_signal: I think if someone was looking for a language line, they probably wouldn't go for the word accessibility because if they didn't speak English, they'd probably look for their language or look for the word language... I think that could be in a different place perhaps.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly stated concern that the current labeling ('accessibility') creates a barrier for non-English speakers who need to locate language support services. The participant identified a significant discoverability issue where the mental model of the target audience does not align with the information architecture. This represents a component task failure (finding language services) that would likely be skipped or worked around by the affected user group.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Non-English speakers and multilingual users would structurally experience higher friction due to the mislabeled pathway; they would search for language-related terms rather than 'accessibility.'

coaching_recommendations.title: Prioritise task-blocking friction before general coaching
coaching_recommendations.summary: The session contains 64 finding-level issue(s), including task-blocking or near-blocking severity (S2).
advice: Review the highest-severity findings before applying general coaching templates.
Prioritise issues that prevented or substantially disrupted task completion.
Use the highest-severity findings to guide focused follow-up coaching.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-A severity distribution: S2=1, S3=6, S4=7, S5=44, S6=6. Representative findings: Participant expressed lack of confidence in choosing a healthcare assessment due to excessive cognitive effort required to compare all options, and indicated intention to use an external tool (ChatGPT) instead of completing the task within the product. | Participant experienced visual discomfort and aversion to the page design due to excessive brightness and color contrast, stating intention to close the page rather than engage with content. | Participant experiences significant visual strain and readability friction from bright white text on the website, which conflicts with their accessibility settings and causes them to avoid reading the content.

coaching_recommendations.title: Multi-pattern friction across 7 categories — anchor coaching on the dominant pattern
coaching_recommendations.summary: This session shows 64 findings spanning 7 distinct friction types (F1,F3,F6,F7,F2,F5,F4). Coaching should follow the dominant pattern first rather than issuing one item per type.
advice: After addressing any severity-priority recommendation first, use this aggregation to group the remaining coaching themes — do not treat the two as parallel.
Treat F1 (Comprehension) as the primary coaching anchor; secondary types (F3 Accessibility, F6 Not Found) provide supporting context.
Avoid issuing parallel recommendations for each friction type when patterns interact within the same session.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-A: total=64 findings across 7 distinct friction types (F1=18, F3=17, F6=9, F7=9, F2=7, F5=3, F4=1).

coaching_recommendations.title: Recording quality is usable but should be improved
coaching_recommendations.summary: The session is still usable for analysis, but defects in the recording may reduce confidence in detailed interpretation.
advice: Check microphone clarity and ambient noise before starting future sessions.
Reduce clipping, brief audio gaps, or interruptions where possible.
Mark this session as usable with caution if fine-grained analysis is required.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-B marks recording quality as acceptable rather than good.
